summary: DHH provides a three-week update following the controversial Basecamp workplace policy changes that banned societal and political discussions at work. He reports that despite a public firestorm and employee departures, the company has regrouped, hired new colleagues, and maintained services without disruption. While HEY saw a small uptick in cancellations, Basecamp saw increased signups, and both products have returned to normal growth. He frames the episode as a storm that has passed and signals no retreat from their positions.
key_points: The business impact of the controversy was minimal — a small uptick in HEY cancellations was offset by increased Basecamp signups
Basecamp quickly regrouped by hiring new colleagues and redistributing work after employee departures
DHH frames the intense public backlash as a temporary storm that inevitably passes
He signals no regret or course correction, doubling down on continuing to share their beliefs publicly
money_quotes: While there was a small uptick in cancelations for HEY during the first tumultuous week, they were more than offset by an increase in new customer signups for Basecamp.
When you're in the midst of a storm like we were, it's easy to temporarily lose hope. To feel like it'll never pass. But it usually does, and so it did at Basecamp.
It hasn't been easy, but that's never deterred us before. And it won't now.
We'll continue to share what we believe and what we've learned. Even when it's hard and even when we make mistakes.
No storm is going to stop that.
key_insight: DHH frames the Basecamp controversy as a weathered storm with minimal business impact, using the quick recovery as validation of their decision to ban workplace political discussions.
